coredata.dc:title: Excellent supercapacitive performance of graphene quantum dots derived from a bio-waste marigold flower (Tagetes erecta)
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC.
coredata.dc:creator: Gupta, Gopal Krishna
Sagar, Pinky
Srivastava, Monika
Singh, Ashwani Kumar
Singh, Jai
Srivastava, S.K.
Srivastava, Amit
coredata.dc:description: Marigold flower (MG; Tagetes erecta) derived Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been successfully reported for the fabrication of supercapacitor electrodes in charge storage devices. The GQDs have been synthesized through a hydrothermal route using biomass viz. Waste material (MG) without adding any hazardous chemicals. The successful formation of GQDs as elaborated has been confirmed by various analytical characterization techniques. The as-synthesized GQDs have been electrodeposited on the Ni foil (working electrode) with the help of PVDF (binder) and subsequently, cyclic voltammetry (CV) has been conducted to access specific capacitance, energy density, and other parameters. Moreover, the galvanometric charge/discharge (GCD) technique has been employed due to its accuracy and reliability. Maximum areal specific capacitance has been found as 1.6008 F/cm2 with the current density of 2.0 A/g even after loading a little amount of material on the electrode. The high magnitude of columbic efficiency (160.08), energy density (17.78 Wh/kg), and specific capacitance of 200 F/g at current density 2.0 A/g within a voltage range of −0.55 V to +0.25 V in 2 M KOH electrolyte solution indicate a good electrocapacitive performance of the as-synthesized material. Moreover, the as-synthesized GQDs have shown excellent capacitive retention after 1000th cycles which clearly embarks its sustainable electrocapacitive nature and henceforth offers outstanding potential for the applications in energy storage devices like supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrode materials
Energy storage device
GQDs
Marigold flowers
Supercapacitor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of GQDs through the hydrothermal method.
(a) X-ray diffraction pattern of as-synthesized MG-GQDs sample and (b) Raman spectrum, of as-synthesized MG-GQDs.

TEM images(a)at bar scale 100 nm and (b) at bar scale 50 nm (Inset describes theSAED pattern of MG-GQDs) (c) HRTEM image at bar scale 10 nm (d) EDSspectrum and elemental analysis of as-synthesis MG-GQDs.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry voltammogram of MG-GQDs electrode at different scan rates (b) Specific capacitance of MG-GQDs at different scan rates, (c) Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves at different current densities (d) Specific capacitance as a function of the current density of MG-GQDs, and (e) Cycling stability of MG-GQDs electrode at current density 2.0 A/g.

(a) Nyquist plot MG- GQDs in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz–0.1 GHz at an AC amplitude of 5 mV in the open circuit potential(b) Bode plot and (c) Ragone plot (power density vs. energy density) for MG-GQDs.
Differentsupercapacitor parameters of the as-synthesized electrode materials as obtained through GCD technique.

head.ce:title: Excellent supercapacitive performance of graphene quantum dots derived from a bio-waste marigold flower (Tagetes erecta)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pure & Applied Physics, Guru Ghasi Das Vishwavidyalaya Bilaspur, 495009, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pure & Applied Physics, Guru Ghasi Das Vishwavidyalaya Bilaspur, 495009, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Marigold flower (MG; Tagetes erecta) derived Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been successfully reported for the fabrication of supercapacitor electrodes in charge storage devices. The GQDs have been synthesized through a hydrothermal route using biomass viz. Waste material (MG) without adding any hazardous chemicals. The successful formation of GQDs as elaborated has been confirmed by various analytical characterization techniques. The as-synthesized GQDs have been electrodeposited on the Ni foil (working electrode) with the help of PVDF (binder) and subsequently, cyclic voltammetry (CV) has been conducted to access specific capacitance, energy density, and other parameters. Moreover, the galvanometric charge/discharge (GCD) technique has been employed due to its accuracy and reliability. Maximum areal specific capacitance has been found as 1.6008 F/cm2 with the current density of 2.0 A/g even after loading a little amount of material on the electrode. The high magnitude of columbic efficiency (160.08), energy density (17.78 Wh/kg), and specific capacitance of 200 F/g at current density 2.0 A/g within a voltage range of −0.55 V to +0.25 V in 2 M KOH electrolyte solution indicate a good electrocapacitive performance of the as-synthesized material. Moreover, the as-synthesized GQDs have shown excellent capacitive retention after 1000th cycles which clearly embarks its sustainable electrocapacitive nature and henceforth offers outstanding potential for the applications in energy storage devices like supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent, carbon-based materials such as activated carbon, graphene oxide, reduced graphene oxide, graphene, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon nitrides, etc., have been explored extensively, as the electrode for charge storage applications like supercapacitor revealing high capacitance [1–5]. However, carbon-based supercapacitor shows poor specific energy density and rate performance caused by its finite on-hand storage sites, which drastically hampers the practical usages [6,7]. GQDs have been regarded as 0D carbon-based novel materials and, attained substantial sincere attention of researchers in materials science. GQDs, basically few-layered graphene along with sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, are crystalline and dimensionally less than 100 nm thick. GQDs are structurally close to the ultimate limit of dimensions i.e. dimension of a single atom [8,9]. It possesses idiosyncratic properties of both quantum dots and graphene and consequently exhibits unique properties such as excellent biocompatibility, aqueous stability, electrical conductivity, high specific surface area (SSA), and friendship with the environment [10,11]. Its good dispersion in various solvents leads to carry different reactions and fastens the electron mobility in solution form. On the other hand, nano-structured size and rich active sites cause the presence of several defects in the honeycombed carbon arrangement of the graphene plane. Therefore, GQDs comprise many properties like high surface area and excellent charge transfer ability derived from edge delocalization and surface defects. Owing to these structural, unique physiochemical properties, they are believed to gain considerable attention as a promising candidate for the fabrication of EDLC supercapacitor as electrode material with excellent rate performance [12–14] in the recent future.

The electronic and size-dependent properties of the GQDs caused by π-π conjugation make them compatible with the alteration of energy states and tuning of sizes for various specific properties [15]. Though there are many methods to synthesize GQDs, two particular processes have gained much attention-bottom-up and top-down methods [13]. Bottom-up methods involve complex steps and chemical reactions. However, the top-down method has been quite easier to follow and does not require any massive post-treatment/procedure. In the following approach, any carbonaceous materials are simply cut down into smaller parts and further treated through the carbonization/hydrothermal approach followed by acid treatment. Pan et al. reported high-quality GQDs with blue photoluminescence by using graphene sheets through the hydrothermal method [16]. Zhang et al. developed GQDs of uniform size via a novel top-down strategy using nitric acid as an oxidant to cut graphene oxide through sonication and hydrothermal approaches [17]. Luo et al. have demonstrated the synthesis of GQDs from Graphite Oxide powder followed by the synthesis of graphene oxide by Hummer's method [18]. Apart from these reports, there have been several studies on the synthesis of GQDs by the green route. Mohan et al. used sugarcane bagasse for the synthesis of GQDs through improvised Hummers' Method followed by carbonization [19]. Suryawanshi et al. have illustrated the large-scale production of GQDs from dead Neem leaves and subsequently used as a metal ion sensor for the detection of Ag+ ions [20]. Ding et al. obtained single-crystal GQDs from lignin carbon waste viz. Biomass followed by oxidized cleavage and aromatic refusion and further implemented for the multicolor bioimaging [21]. The previous studies suggest that there has been a huge surge in the green synthesis of these materials leading to the cost-effective and eco-friendly synthesis such as the synthesis of carbon-based materials from sustainable source peanut shells, pistachio shells, capsicum, sweet potato, rice husk, etc., [22–28]. However, the synthesis of GQDs with high surface area and high charge storage capacity has been still a challenge.

Herein, we developed a novel and simple strategy to synthesize GQDs using eco-friendly biomass Marigold Flower (Tagetes erecta) as a carbon precursor and without using any hazardous chemical through the hydrothermal approach. Further, the as-synthesized GQDs have been used as an electrode material for the fabrication of energy storage device applications. The various functional group-rich surfaces of GQDs offer the fast electron transfer, flexibility, and improved performance of the supercapacitor.

The sustainable source Marigold flower (Tagetes erecta) as a natural precursor, was collected from the premises of Botanical garden of Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi- India. N-Dimethyl formamide (N-DMF), nickel foam, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) were procured from Sigma Aldrich, USA while sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium hydroxide (KOH) and concentrated Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was supplied by Alfa Aesar, India, and used without modification. Deionized water was used for the preparation of all solutions required during characterizations and experimental procedures.

We adopted a one-hand simple facile hydrothermal method for the synthesis of GQDs. Marigold (MG) flowers were collected from the premises of BHU India, and followed by the separation of petals and seeds. Further, MG petals were washed thoroughly with DI water 3 times and left to be dried at room temperature. As-obtained MG petals were carbonized in a Muffle furnace at 400 ֯C and ground with the help of a pestle and mortar. The as-carbonized MG powder was washed with 50 mL of 0.1 M HCl solution. Furthermore, the powder was dispersed in DI water and the respective pH∼ 7 was maintained followed by ultrasonication for 30 min at room temperature. Next, 10 mL of 1 M NaOH was added to the above solution under vigorous stirring for 30 min at room temperature. Further, the mixture was poured into a 100 mL capacity stainless steel autoclave with a Teflon vessel and held in a furnace at 180 °C for 12 h, the autoclave was allowed to cool down to room temperature, and subsequently, the brownish concentrated solution was collected. The as-obtained product was further centrifuged and the precipitate was washed with DI water/ethanol in triplicate to ensure the complete removal of ions and other possible residuals, followed by drying in a vacuum oven at 65 °C overnight. The GQDs were finally collected as a brownish powder for further studies.

For the fabrication of a working electrode, the active material (90 wt %), and (PVDF) binder (10 wt %) were dispersed in N-DMF solvent. The purpose of adding the binder is to keep the active material attached to the electrode in the electrode solution. The solution was stirred for 1 h for a homogeneous mixture. Subsequently, the prepared solution mixture was coated on the working electrode (Ni foil; 0.5 × 0.5 cm2) followed by drying at 70 ֠C for 5 h in a vacuum oven. The total mass loaded on the electrode was ∼2 mg. Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were conducted in 2 M KOH electrolyte with a potential range from −0.55 V to +0.25 V.

The structural features and the crystalline properties of the as-synthesized powder sample were studied by X-ray powder diffraction method, through X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical X'Pert PRO) using CuKα radiation (λ∼1.5406 Å at 40 kV and 30 mA), in 2θ range of 10°–80°. The microstructures and morphologies were analyzed by transmission electron microscope (TEM; Technai G2,F 20- Twin, Swiss Republic)operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The elemental analysis was done by the EDAX (Energy-dispersive X-ray) attachment. The room temperature vibrational characteristics of the samples were carried out with a 532 nm Nd: YAG laser excitation using a micro- Raman spectrometer (Renishaw inVia Raman Microscope) in a wavenumber range of 300–3000 cm−1. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) were recorded To get information of the functional groups attached to the as-synthesized sample, by a BRUKER-ALPHA II FT-IR spectrometer at room temperature. The optical properties were evaluated by UV–Visible absorption and PL measurements using PerkinElmer UV–Visible spectrometer and PerkinElmer Lambda 25 luminescence spectrophotometer, respectively. To access the electrochemical performances, cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charging-discharging (GCD) measurements, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis were performed on an electrochemical work station (CHI-660C, UK) along with a three-electrode system.

The Electrochemical performances of the as-synthesized sample were measured on a CHI-660C electrochemical work station by a three electrode-based system. For the perception of Ag/AgCl, a potential difference was recorded considering it as the reference electrode. Platinum wire and Ni foam were used as a counter electrode and working electrode, respectively. All the electrochemical measurements were done in 2 M KOH (alkaline electrolyte) at room temperature.

CV measurements were performed at various scan rates (10–300 mV/s) in operational potential window −0.55 V to +0.25 V. The frequency range for EIS tests was selected from 0.01 Hz to 0.1 GHz at an AC amplitude of 5 mV in the open circuit potential. Furthermore, various electrochemical variables have been accessed with the help of the following relations [29,30].

The specific capacitance (Ccv) as
(1)
C cv
=
∫ I dv
ν × m × ΔV
( F/g )

And areal capacitance (Cs) as
(2)
C s
=
I x Δt
ΔV x ν
(
F/cm 2
)
here, I, m,ΔV, Δt, and ν represent the current, mass, voltage window, discharge time of the active material (MG-GQDs), and scan rate (v/s), respectively.

First, we have performed all the measurements using the CV technique. Other researchers documented that the GCD technique comparatively gives more accurate results, and hence more reliable. In the light of the above view, we have switched to the GCD technique for further measurements and subsequent calculations. Therefore, the specific capacitance can be calculated with the following relation [30,31]:
(3)
C m
=
I x Δt
ΔV x m
( F/g )
here, the symbols have their usual meaning as described above.

Moreover, energy density (E) power density (P) and Columbic efficiency (
η
) have been estimated through the following relations:
(4)
E =
c s
× Δ
V 2
7.2
(
Wh / kg
)
(5)
P =
E × 3600
Δ t
(
W / kg
)
(6)
η =
t D
t c
x 100
here, tD and tC denote the discharging and charging time, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray diffraction, TEM, EDS, FT-IR, and Raman spectra
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 1 shows a schematic illustration of the successful synthesis of GQDs from a sustainable source as a precursor Marigold flower (Tagetes erecta) through the hydrothermal method. Fig. 2 shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the as-synthesized GQDs. A typical broad peak at ∼21.2° can be assigned to MG-GQDs [32]. Two other peaks appeared at 31.4° and 45.1° correspond to (100) and (002) reflection planes of hexagonal carbon (JCPDS No. 41-1487), respectively. The average crystallite size has been estimated as ∼5.7 nm through the Debye-Scherrer formula (equation (7)) [33]:
(7)
D =
0.9 λ
βCos
( θ )
here, D represents the crystallite size (nm), λ is the wavelength of the X-ray (nm) employed, θ is the Bragg diffraction angle (degree) &β denotes the FWHM (nm).

The other structural features of MG-GQDs have been analyzed by Raman spectroscopy. Raman spectra of the as-synthesized MG-GQDs have been represented in Fig. 2(b). It exhibits two characteristic peaks at 1365 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 which correspond to D- and G-bands of graphene, respectively [34]. The D-peak gets originated from the disordered band structure of k-point phonons of A1g symmetry of carbon atoms leading to the in-plane vibration of hexagonal graphitic layers [35]. This confirms the presence of defects due to the residual functional groups in the as-synthesized samples. The G-peak attributes to the E2g phonon at the Brillouin zone center, indicating the crystallinity of the graphitized carbon network [36]. The presence of an additional peak at 2708 cm−1 (2D band) reveals the graphene nature of carbon material [37]. The relative intensity of the disorder band to the crystalline band (ID/IG) has been estimated as 0.86, signifying the high structural regularity of the as-synthesized MG-GQDs.

TEM studies verify the successful synthesis of GQDs from a natural precursor and provide morphological insights of the as-synthesized sample MG-GQDs and shown in Fig. 3 (a). Fig. 3(a and b) demonstrates that as-synthesized MG-GQDs possess a typical spherical shape. Further, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern as shown in Fig. 3(b) (inset) reveals the amorphous nature of the as-synthesized MG-GQDs. The inset of Fig. 3(b) confirms that the reflection planes match well with (102) and (100) planes of hexagonal carbon. HRTEM image demonstrates lattice spacing as ∼0.22 nm corresponding to (100) reflection plane confirming the formation of GQDs [32](Fig. 3(c)). Fig. 3(d) shows the Energy-dispersive spectrum (EDS) of marigold-derived GQDs and illustrates the presence of carbon, oxygen, and sodium content and their corresponding weight/atomic percentages.

The FT-IR spectroscopy was performed to reveal the chemical environment of the as-synthesized MG-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , there have been various functional groups present over the surface of the as-synthesized MG-GQDs which may facilitate the electron transfer. The appearance of peaks at around ∼1707 cm−1and ∼1574 cm−1 confirms the bending modes of CO and CC groups, respectively. A broad absorption band at ∼3000-3500 cm−1 owes to –OH and –NH functional groups [32,38]. Further, the presence of the hydroxyl (O–H) group demonstrates the hydrophilic nature and good solubility of as-synthesized MG-GQDs in water. The absorption bands at ∼2930 cm−1 and ∼2844 cm−1 are related to the stretching of C–H (alkane group). Moreover, other vibrational bands at ∼1400, ∼1023 cm−1 assigned to the carboxylic group, C–N stretching, respectively while CC bending appears at ∼839 cm−1 [32,39].

Further, the formation of the GQDs derived from the marigold flower has been confirmed by UV–Visible absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy (Fig. S1 (a) and (b)). It has been observed that the MG-GQDs appear fluorescent blue when subjected to light with wavelength, λ ∼365 nm. A related brief discussion has been added to the electronic supporting information.

The capacitive performances of the as-synthesized marigold derived GQDs have been evaluated by CV and GCD methods using a three-electrode system. The CV experiment embellishes the relevant information regarding the charging/discharging behavior of the as-synthesized MG-GQDs. Fig. 5 (a) delineates perfect rectangular curves without any redox peak for 0.01 V/s to 0.30 V/s scan rate. This phenomenon exhibits good supercapacitor behavior of electrochemical double-layer capacitor (EDLC) electrode in which the energy storage mechanism relies on reversible ion adsorption-desorption of electrolyte ions and the presence of –OH groups on the surface of the electrode [40]. Due to ultrafast carrier transport at the electrode surface during the charging/discharging processes, the prepared electrode shows the distortion of the CV curve at a high scan rate [40,41].

The specific capacity of the MG-GQDs can be calculated using equation (1). Fig. 5(b) illustrates that the maximum specific capacity as 205.8 F g−1 from the CV measurements at 0.01 V/s scan rate. Further, it has been observed that the specific capacitance decreases with a higher scan rate due to fast transfer for ions for a smalltime. Furthermore, we have deduced the energy density with the help of equation (3) and subsequently the highest energy density as 18.29 Wh/Kg at 0.01 V/s scan rate (Table S1).

To exemplify the Columbia efficiency, electrochemical areal, and gravimetric capacitance, we have used the galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) technique (Table 1 ). Fig. 5(c) represents the GCD curves for as-synthesized MG-GQDs. Nearly symmetrical triangular curves at different current densities in the range 2.0 A/g to 10 A/g stipulate marvelous reversibility for EDLC behavior and exhibit linear potential-time curves, indicating fast current-voltage response in the electron transfer process [39].

Fig. 5(c) and (d) depict the highest areal specific capacitance as 1.6008 F/cm2 and the gravimetric specific capacitance as 200 F/g at 2.0 A/g current density for as-synthesized MG-GQDs as calculated through equations (2) and (3), respectively. The specific capacitance increases at a lower current density as more electrolyte ions can filter inside the pores of the electrode. Columbic efficiency has been determined through the ratio of charging and discharging times of the respective material using equation (6). The respective analysis has been displayed in Table 1 which illustrates that the discharging time is greater than charging time with Columbic efficiency ∼160.08 at current density 2.0 A/g and henceforth confirms a superior supercapacitor electrode. Generally, all the Columbic efficiency has been calculated from the first cycle which determines the stability and sustainability of this material. Moreover, the cyclic stability of the electrode material has been regarded as an important parameter to validate the practical implications of the fabricated supercapacitor. Fig. 5(e) displays the cyclability of the as-synthesized electrode material which infer its ability for fast charging and discharging without hardly any degradation even after the 1000th cycle. It primarily confirms the durability of the as-prepared electrode material.

Furthermore, the Ragone plot for the supercapacitor electrode materials has been traced (Fig. 6 (c)). It shows a maximum energy density of 17.78 Wh/kg with a reasonably good power density of 799.70 W/kg at the fixed potential window as shown in Fig. 5(c). The energy density and power density have been deduced through equations (4) and (5), respectively. It clearly shows much better energy density without immolating the power density nearly similar to previously reported carbon-based supercapacitor [42–46].

To investigate the internal resistance between the working electrode and electrolyte, EIS measurements were carried out and the results have been analyzed using Nyquist and Bode plots. The intersection of the curve and the horizontal axis represents the total electric resistance of the device. The diameter of the semicircle at high frequency corresponds to the charge transfer resistance between the electrode material and an electrolyte, and tail slope at low frequency attributes to the ionic diffusion rate in the electrolyte [47–49]. Fig. 6(a) illustrates the Nyquist plot, in which – Z ̋ (imaginary part) has been plotted as a function of Z' (real part)in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 0.1 GHz at an AC amplitude of 5 mV in the open circuit potential. Subsequently, the electric resistance of as-synthesized MG-GQDs for the proposed supercapacitor has been found as 4.65 Ω.

In summary, the present study illustrates a cost-effective, environment-friendly green synthesis of a sustainable supercapacitor electrode material GQDs from a natural precursor marigold flower through hydrothermal approach. The as-synthesized electrode material achieves remarkably good electrocapacitive performance with specific capacitance of 200 F/g at a current density 2.0 A/g. Moreover, the as-synthesized GQDs have shown excellent capacitive retention after 1000 cycles which clearly embarks its sustainable electrocapacitive nature. The obtained capacitive electrode material delivers an ultrahigh energy density of 17.78 Wh/kg with a reasonably good power density of 799.70 W/kg and henceforth offers an outstanding potential for the practical applications in energy storage devices like supercapacitors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to the Bio-Physics Lab, Department of Physics, BHU- Varanasi and CIF, IIT (BHU)- Varanasi for the access to various characterization/measurement facilities employed in the present study. Authors (GKP & AS) express their gratitude to the Vice Chancellor, VBS Purvanchal University for all the required supports in the research work. Ms. Pinky sagar acknowledges to DST-New Delhi for providing INSPIRE Fellowship (DST/INSPIRE/03/2018/000041). One of the authors (SKS) is thankful to IoE (Institution of eminence), BHU for providing the financial support.
